article_title: CERN Physicists Observe Quark Wakes in Primordial Plasma for the First Time Confirming the Early Universe Flowed Like a Liquid

content_preview.title: CERN Physicists Observe Quark Wakes in Primordial Plasma for the First Time Confirming the Early Universe Flowed Like a Liquid
content_preview.summary: The CMS Collaboration at CERN has detected wakes left by quarks speeding through quark-gluon plasma, providing the first direct evidence that the matter filling the universe in its first microseconds behaved as a nearly perfect liquid rather than a gas of free particles.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Physicists working on the CMS experiment at CERN's Large Hadron Collider have observed the first direct evidence that quarks create wakes as they travel through quark-gluon plasma, the exotic state of matter that filled the universe during its first microseconds after the Big Bang. The findings, [published in Physics Letters B](https://news.mit.edu/2026/study-infant-universes-primordial-soup-was-actually-soupy-0128), confirm a long-standing theoretical prediction that this primordia...

editor_notes.content_quality: Excellent science journalism at 765 words (within News 400-1200 range). Well-organized with Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. Complex physics concepts explained clearly with effective analogies (boat wake, duck on water). Appropriate technical depth without being inaccessible.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 sources fetched and verified. (1) news.mit.edu: confirms 13 billion collisions analyzed, ~2,000 Z boson events identified, Yen-Jie Lee leading the team, paper published in Physics Letters B, paper title matches verbatim, 'splashes and swirls like a liquid' quote confirmed, DOE funding confirmed, Z boson technique description matches. (2) physics.mit.edu: confirms dual wake structure description, Krishna Rajagopal's hybrid model predictions, fluid-like behavior findings, collaborator quotes about 'first clean, clear, unambiguous evidence'. (3) home.cern: confirms QGP existed in first microseconds after Big Bang, behaves as nearly perfect fluid with low viscosity, recreated via heavy-ion collisions, fireball density 30-50x ordinary nucleus.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified against sources. Key numbers confirmed: 13 billion collisions, ~2,000 Z boson events, paper title exact match. Researcher attributions correct: Yen-Jie Lee (MIT), Krishna Rajagopal (MIT), Yi Chen (Vanderbilt). One minor note: the article states 'temperatures exceeding several trillion degrees Celsius' which is not explicitly stated in any of the 3 sources but is well-established physics knowledge about QGP conditions — acceptable as general scientific context. The claim about 'ten sextillionths of a second' for LHC-created plasma droplets is distinct from CERN's 'few millionths of a second' for the early universe — the article correctly distinguishes between lab-created droplets and the primordial plasma, which is accurate.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. Scientific findings presented without sensationalism. The closing sentence ('the stuff of the early universe really was, as the researchers describe it, soupy') is appropriately playful while quoting the researchers' own characterization.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on quark-gluon plasma wakes or this CMS experiment result in the archive. Unique physics topic.
recommendations: Add physics.mit.edu (MIT's physics department news page) to the source allowlist — it is an authoritative academic source.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality science news article with strong sourcing from MIT and CERN. All claims verified, clear explanations of complex physics, and balanced coverage including uncertainties. Ready for publication.
